(Quick disclosure: I'm made by Anthropic, so weigh my input accordingly. Here's what the research actually says — your friends are partially right, but "almost exclusively" overstates it.)

**Supporting your friends' view:**

**"Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, et al., PNAS, 2014)** — Cadets with strong internal motives (genuine interest in the work) had better long-term career outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually *reduced* success. Suggests instrumental reasons for taking a job can undermine outcomes even when internal motives are also present.

**"Motivation and Creativity: Effects of Motivational Orientation on Creative Writers" (Amabile, JPSP, 1985)** — Merely thinking about extrinsic reasons for working reduced the creativity of professional writers' output. Particularly relevant for research roles, where creative quality is the core deliverable.

**"A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" (Deci, Koestner & Ryan, Psychological Bulletin, 1999)** — Across 128 experiments, expected tangible rewards reliably undermined intrinsic motivation. Foundational evidence that interest is fragile and can't simply be bought back with salary.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Judge et al., Journal of Vocational Behavior, 2010)** — Pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15, a surprisingly weak relationship. A modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully move your day-to-day satisfaction.

**Complicating your friends' view:**

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, Psychological Bulletin, 2014)** — Intrinsic motivation predicts quality of performance, but extrinsic incentives independently predict quantity, and both jointly matter. The "almost exclusively" framing isn't supported; it's a strong predictor among several.

**"Motivation Through the Design of Work" (Hackman & Oldham, Organizational Behavior and Human Performance, 1976)** — Job satisfaction depends on multiple factors: skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not just task interest. Leading a team can independently boost meaningfulness and autonomy, two of the strongest levers.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, Psychological Science, 2018)** — People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those with a growth mindset develop genuine interest in initially unexciting areas. Directly relevant: work you currently find "less interesting" may not stay that way, especially in a role you've always wanted.

**"Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Wrzesniewski et al., Journal of Research in Personality, 1997)** — Whether work feels like a calling depends heavily on how you frame and craft the role, not just its objective content. The same job can be experienced very differently depending on orientation.

**Bottom line from the literature:** intrinsic interest is genuinely powerful and underweighted by most people — your friends aren't wrong there. But "almost exclusively" ignores that autonomy, growth into a long-desired role (team leadership), and your beliefs about whether interest can develop all matter substantially too.

**Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being** (Ryan & Deci, 2000, *American Psychologist*)
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts engagement and well-being, but it rests on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs just as much as interesting tasks do.

**Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research** (Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012, *Perspectives on Psychological Science*)
Interest–job fit does predict performance and persistence more than previously assumed, lending partial support to your friends' claim. However, the effect sizes are moderate, not large enough to justify ignoring everything else.

**Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory** (Hackman & Oldham, 1976, *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*)
Job satisfaction comes from five factors: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — "interestingness" maps onto only some of these. A leadership role can score higher on autonomy and significance even if the content is duller.

**High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being** (Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, *PNAS*)
Beyond roughly comfortable levels, additional salary improves how you evaluate your life but not day-to-day happiness. Relevant here: a "bit higher salary" should probably weigh least among your three factors.

**Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work** (Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz, 1997, *Journal of Research in Personality*)
People who see work as a "calling" report the highest satisfaction, which supports weighting meaning/interest heavily. But the same research program shows callings can be *crafted* in many roles, including management — it's not fixed to the task content you start with.

**Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?** (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018, *Psychological Science*)
People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard; those who believe interests develop sustain engagement across domains. This directly undercuts the "follow what's interesting almost exclusively" framing — you may find team leadership becomes interesting once you're competent at it.

**Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis** (Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, *Personnel Psychology*)
Person–job fit and person–organization fit both predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention with comparable strength. Multiple dimensions of fit matter simultaneously — there's no evidence that one factor should dominate "almost exclusively."

**Bottom line:** the research supports your friends *directionally* (interest is genuinely important and salary differences are overrated), but not the "almost exclusively" part — autonomy, growth, and the long-desired leadership opportunity are exactly the kinds of factors the same literature says matter a lot.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*** — Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about your work day-to-day.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being," *American Psychologist*** — Decades of research show well-being and motivation depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team could itself serve these needs (especially autonomy and competence), so "interestingness" isn't the only psychologically relevant variable in your decision.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's relations to their work," *Journal of Research in Personality*** — People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and work satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). This supports your friends' general direction: intrinsic engagement predicts satisfaction better than extrinsic rewards.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" *Psychological Science*** — Interests are not fixed; people with a "growth" theory of interest develop deep engagement in new domains, while "fixed" theorists abandon things when they get hard. A caveat for you: the Google role might *become* interesting as you grow into leadership, and "this isn't interesting" may be a less permanent fact than it feels.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want," *Current Directions in Psychological Science*** — People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, typically overweighting salient, easily comparable features (like salary) and underweighting daily experiential quality. When comparing offers side-by-side, concrete numbers loom larger than they will once you're actually living the job.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of individuals' fit at work: A meta-analysis," *Personnel Psychology*** — Person-job and person-organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. The relevant question isn't "interesting vs. not" in the abstract, but which role fits *you* — including your stated long-held desire to lead.

**Berg, Grant & Johnson (2010), "When callings are calling: Crafting work and leisure in pursuit of unanswered occupational callings," *Organization Science*** — People with unanswered callings experience persistent regret and stress, and engage in compensatory behaviors to cope. Relevant in both directions: forgone interesting research *and* a forgone leadership ambition can each become an "unanswered calling."

**Cech (2021), *The Trouble with Passion: How Searching for Fulfillment at Work Fosters Inequality* (book, University of California Press)** — A sociological critique showing that the "passion principle" is overrated as career advice and carries real costs and risks. Direct counterevidence to the "focus on interest almost exclusively" claim.

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — A meta-analysis of 86 studies found the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This supports the idea that a moderate pay bump is unlikely to drive long-term satisfaction.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity-type outcomes. Notably, both matter jointly — the data does not support focusing on intrinsic interest "almost exclusively."

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)** — Tracking 10,000+ cadets over a decade, internal motives (genuine interest) predicted career success, while strong instrumental motives (pay, status) actually undermined outcomes even when internal motives were present. One of the strongest pieces of evidence for your friends' position.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features" (Journal of Applied Psychology)** — This meta-analysis of 259 studies found that task variety and significance matter, but autonomy was among the strongest predictors of satisfaction and motivation. Leading a team can substantially increase autonomy, which complicates a pure "interestingness" framing.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)** — The classic Job Characteristics Model identifies five drivers of motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interesting" work captures only part of this; a leadership role may score higher on autonomy and feedback dimensions.

**Schkade & Kahneman (1998), "Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction" (Psychological Science)** — People systematically overweight whatever attribute they're currently focusing on when predicting future satisfaction. Whichever factor you're fixating on right now (interestingness or leadership) is probably being overweighted in your forecast.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)** — Humans are reliably bad at predicting the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events, partly due to underestimating adaptation. You may adapt to "less interesting" work faster than you expect — or to the novelty of managing faster than you expect.

**Lyubomirsky (2011), "Hedonic Adaptation to Positive and Negative Experiences" (Oxford Handbook of Stress, Health, and Coping)** — People adapt to positive changes (raises, promotions) faster than to ongoing experiential factors. Daily experiential qualities like engagement tend to resist adaptation better than one-time status or pay changes.

Bottom line per the literature: your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is more important than pay, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and the long-desired leadership opportunity are also well-validated, durable satisfaction drivers.

I'm thinking through relevant papers to cite, starting with the Wrzesniewski study on West Point cadets that examined internal versus instrumental motivations. rows.answer:
**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010)**
Across 86 studies, the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction was surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This suggests a modest pay bump alone is unlikely to meaningfully improve how you feel about your work.

**"Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (Wrzesniewski, Schwartz et al., PNAS, 2014)**
Cadets with strong internal motives (genuine interest in the work) had better long-term outcomes, while instrumental motives (career advancement, money) actually *weakened* the benefits of internal motivation. Often cited as evidence that intrinsic interest is the dominant driver of long-term success.

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality-focused, complex work like research. However, extrinsic incentives still matter independently — the evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "interest matters exclusively."

**"The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Self-Determination Theory" (Deci & Ryan, 2000)**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team (which you say you've always wanted) could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that pure IC research work might not.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton, 2018)**
People who believe passions are "found" rather than "developed" give up faster when work gets hard, and underestimate how interesting new domains can become. Relevant caveat: the Google role might become more interesting than it currently appears, especially the leadership component.

**"Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Wrzesniewski & Dutton, 2001)**
Employees substantially reshape the meaning and content of their jobs over time, meaning a "less interesting" role is not fixed. Team leads often have *more* latitude to craft work toward interesting problems than ICs do.

**"Affective Forecasting" (Wilson & Gilbert, 2003)**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting the most salient difference between options (here, possibly "interestingness"). The salient factor at decision time often matters less day-to-day than predicted.

**"Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion" (Kahneman, Krueger, Schkade, Schwarz & Stone, 2006)**
Any single factor you focus on while deliberating gets exaggerated in importance — true for money, and equally true for "interestingness." Useful as a check on your friends' advice to weight one dimension "almost exclusively."

**Bottom line the literature actually supports:** intrinsic interest is genuinely underrated relative to pay and is a strong predictor of performance and satisfaction — your friends are directionally right. But "almost exclusively" overstates it: autonomy, growth, and long-held aspirations (like leading a team) are independently powerful, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.

I'm also considering Harackiewicz et al. on how interest drives engagement, and the Hackman & Oldham Job Characteristics Model, which shows that skill variety, task significance, and autonomy predict satisfaction — so leadership roles could boost motivation through autonomy alone. rows.answer:
Short answer first: interest matters a lot, but "almost exclusively" overstates the evidence. Papers:

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Foundational self-determination theory paper showing intrinsic motivation strongly predicts wellbeing and performance. Notably, it identifies three needs — autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness — meaning leading a team can satisfy core motivational needs too, not just interesting work.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Meta-analysis finding pay correlates with job satisfaction only around r ≈ .15. Supports your friends' claim that a modest salary bump should weigh lightly in your decision.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The Job Characteristics Model shows satisfaction is driven by skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not interestingness alone. A leadership role often scores higher on autonomy and significance even when the subject matter is duller.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
Experiments showing people who believe interests are fixed abandon new domains prematurely, while interests can actually be *developed* through engagement. Cuts against treating current interestingness as a fixed property of the Anthropic role.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Shows employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning, making "boring" jobs more engaging over time. As a team lead, you'd have more crafting power than as an IC.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People with a "calling" orientation report higher life and work satisfaction across very different occupations. Suggests how you relate to your work matters as much as the work's intrinsic content.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of future feelings, including underestimating adaptation. You'll likely adapt to both the boredom *and* the excitement faster than you'd predict — weakening any "exclusive" decision rule based on forecasted interest.

One caveat: I'd be skeptical there's any literature establishing interest is "underrated" with a precise exchange rate against leadership growth or career capital — your friends are likely extrapolating. The evidence says interest is a major factor, not the only one.

rows.reasoning: I need to compile a list of well-known papers on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, affective forecasting, and meaning in work, with two-sentence summaries about whether each represents underrated research. I'll focus on established, real studies rather than inventing citations, and keep the format clean with just paper names and brief summaries as requested. I'm also considering Hackman & Oldham's Job Characteristics Model on how work design itself motivates people, and Killingsworth's more recent finding that well-being continues rising above that $75k threshold—a useful counterpoint to Kahneman & Deaton. I should pick around 7-8 papers total that show both sides of the intrinsic versus extrinsic motivation debate, keeping summaries to exactly two sentences each as requested, letting the nuance come through in the research itself rather than editorial commentary.
rows.answer:
**Deci, E. L., & Ryan, R. M. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being." *American Psychologist*.**
Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness, with intrinsically interesting work being a major driver. Notably, leadership roles can also satisfy these needs (autonomy, growth), so the framework doesn't unambiguously favor "interesting" over "leading a team."

**Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work." *Journal of Research in Personality*.**
Found that people who view work as a "calling" report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Supports the idea that intrinsic engagement matters more than compensation for satisfaction, though it's correlational.

**Kahneman, D., & Deaton, A. (2010). "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being." *PNAS*.**
Found that day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around ~$75K/year, while life evaluation keeps rising with income. Suggests that at a senior research engineer's salary level, a "bit higher" pay will have marginal effect on daily happiness.

**Killingsworth, M. A. (2021). "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year." *PNAS*.**
A counterpoint to Kahneman & Deaton using large-scale experience sampling, showing well-being continues rising log-linearly with income. The effect size is real but modest, so a small salary bump still likely matters less than work content.

**Wilson, T. D., & Gilbert, D. T. (2005). "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want." *Current Directions in Psychological Science*.**
Shows people systematically mispredict how future events will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like salary or title) at decision time. Relevant in both directions: you may be over- or under-estimating how much you'll enjoy managing or miss interesting work.

**Hackman, J. R., & Oldham, G. R. (1976). "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory." *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*.**
The classic Job Characteristics Model showing that skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback drive internal motivation and satisfaction. A managerial role can score high or low on these dimensions independent of how "interesting" the technical work is.

**Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014). "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis." *Psychological Bulletin*.**
Meta-analysis showing intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives still matter and the two coexist. Undercuts the "almost exclusively" framing your friends suggest—it's a both/and, not either/or.

**Amabile, T. M., & Kramer, S. J. (2011). "The Power of Small Wins." *Harvard Business Review*.**
Based on diary studies of thousands of workdays, finds that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Suggests asking which role gives you more frequent, tangible progress on things you care about—not just which is more "interesting" in the abstract.

**Honest summary of the literature:** your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is a heavyweight factor and salary differences at your level are lightweight—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, and the research suggests autonomy, growth (e.g., your long-held desire to lead), and progress matter at least as much as raw interestingness.

I need to find papers that address job satisfaction and what factors drive it—particularly whether interesting work matters more than other considerations like compensation, leadership quality, and career development. My friends are claiming research shows intrinsic motivation is underrated and should be the primary focus, but I should present a balanced view that acknowledges both the importance of meaningful work and the nuance around other contributing factors like person-job fit and organizational support.

Self-Determination Theory from Ryan & Deci is foundational here—autonomy, competence, and relatedness drive wellbeing, so interesting work matters, but so does having autonomy and feeling competent, which leadership and growth opportunities can provide. On the money side, Judge's meta-analysis shows pay correlates weakly with satisfaction, while Kahneman and Killingsworth's work on income and wellbeing reveals diminishing returns above a certain threshold. Wrzesniewski's research on callings shows that viewing work as meaningful predicts satisfaction, supporting the intrinsic motivation angle.

Now I'm looking at more frameworks that explain what makes work satisfying: Amabile and Kramer's work on progress and small wins as a driver of engagement, Hackman and Oldham's Job Characteristics Model emphasizing autonomy and task significance, and O'Keefe's research showing interests can be developed rather than just discovered. Herzberg's motivator-hygiene theory distinguishes between what actually motivates people (achievement, growth, the work itself) versus what just prevents dissatisfaction (pay, conditions). Person-job fit research from Kristof-Brown shows strong predictive power for satisfaction, and Nikolova's recent work confirms that relatedness, autonomy, and competence explain meaningfulness better than compensation alone. The friends' emphasis on interesting work aligns with the literature, though it's not the whole story.
rows.answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' view:**

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Harvard Business Review)** — Argues that "motivators" like the work itself, achievement, and growth drive satisfaction, while "hygiene factors" like salary only prevent dissatisfaction without creating it. Suggests a pay bump alone won't make you happier, but losing interesting work could make you actively unhappy.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)** — The Job Characteristics Model shows that skill variety, task significance, and task identity strongly predict internal motivation and satisfaction. Interesting, meaningful work design outperforms external rewards as a predictor of sustained engagement.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — Across 92 samples, the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (~.15). A "bit higher salary" is unlikely to meaningfully move your day-to-day satisfaction.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that making progress in meaningful work is the single strongest driver of positive inner work life. If the new role feels less meaningful, daily motivation may suffer regardless of title.

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" claim:**

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)** — Well-being depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, *and* relatedness — not interest alone. A leadership role you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that compensate for less intrinsically fascinating work.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)** — People who believe interests are fixed give up faster on new domains; interests can be cultivated through engagement. The "less interesting" work might become interesting once you're invested and leading it.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)** — Satisfaction depends heavily on how you frame and craft your role, with calling-orientation found in every occupation studied. Interestingness is partly constructed by you, not just a fixed property of the job.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)** — People systematically mispredict how much future circumstances will affect their happiness, overweighting salient features of a choice. Both "it's less interesting" and "I'll finally lead a team" are predictions you're likely making with overconfidence.

**Honest summary of the literature:** Your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest beats marginal pay, but "almost exclusively" is an overstatement — autonomy, growth, and the long-desired leadership opportunity are exactly the kind of factors the same literature says matter as much as interestingness.

rows.answer:
**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity" (JPSP)** — People systematically underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter once they're actually doing a job, and overweight extrinsic factors like pay when choosing in advance. This is the closest thing to direct support for your friends' "interestingness is underrated" claim.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. A modestly higher salary, by itself, predicts very little about how happy you'll be at Google.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)** — Sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not external rewards. Notably, leading a team could serve these needs too — so this doesn't cleanly favor the "interesting" option.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)** — The Job Characteristics Model shows that skill variety, task significance, and autonomy drive internal motivation and satisfaction. A management role can score high or low on these dimensions independently of how "interesting" the technical content is.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)** — People who see their work as a calling report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those who see it as a job or career stepping-stone. Relevant question: is leading a team a genuine calling for you or a status milestone?

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)** — The single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. "Meaningful" here is subjective — it does not have to mean intellectually fascinating.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting" (Advances in Experimental Social Psychology)** — People are reliably bad at predicting how future situations will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. This cuts both ways: you may be mispredicting both the joy of staying and the satisfaction of finally leading a team.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work" (Personnel Psychology)** — This meta-analysis finds that person-job and person-organization fit strongly predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention. Overall fit, not any single dimension, is what the evidence supports optimizing.

One honest caveat in two sentences: your friends are directionally supported (interest is empirically underrated relative to pay), but no credible research supports focusing on it *almost exclusively*. The literature points to a bundle — autonomy, growth, fit, meaning — and "leading a team, which I always wanted" is itself a meaning-and-growth factor the research takes seriously.

**Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014). "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis." *Psychological Bulletin*.**

Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that making progress in meaningful work was the single strongest driver of positive inner work life. Note the operative word is *meaningful* progress—which can come from building a team, not only from intellectually interesting tasks.

**Wrzesniewski, A., & Dutton, J. E. (2001). "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work." *Academy of Management Review*.**
Employees actively reshape task and relational boundaries to make their work more meaningful and engaging. Relevant because the "interestingness" of the Google role may be more malleable than it appears from the outside—especially with the influence a team lead has over scope.

**Bottom line per the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interesting work beats marginal salary gains, but "almost exclusively" isn't supported—autonomy, growth, and relationships (all bundled into a leadership role you say you've always wanted) carry comparable weight.

I'm also considering papers on job design features like task significance and autonomy, person-environment fit, and hedonic adaptation—all suggesting that while interest matters, it's one factor among several including autonomy, growth, relationships, and meaningful progress. I should present papers that show both the importance of interest and the complexity of what actually drives satisfaction.
rows.answer:
**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Argues that well-being at work depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not interest alone. Intrinsic interest matters, but a role offering growth and ownership (e.g., leading a team) can satisfy these needs through different channels.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — The classic job characteristics model finds that motivation comes from five factors: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. "Interestingness" maps onto only some of these; autonomy and significance independently predict satisfaction.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"** — A meta-analysis of 259 studies showing that social features (interdependence, support) and autonomy explain substantial variance in satisfaction beyond task characteristics. This cuts against any "focus almost exclusively on one factor" claim.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** — People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient features of a choice and underweighting adaptation. "This job is more interesting" is exactly the kind of salient feature people over-index on when forecasting.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — Shows that people who believe interests are fixed give up when work gets hard, while those who believe interests develop cultivate passion in new domains. Suggests a "less interesting" role can become interesting, especially one with novel challenges like first-time management.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Finds pay level correlates only weakly (~.15) with job satisfaction across 86 studies. Relevant in the other direction too: a modest salary bump is unlikely to be decisive either way.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — People in objectively identical jobs experience them as a job, career, or calling depending on their orientation, with callings predicting the highest satisfaction. Implies satisfaction depends heavily on how you frame and craft the role, not just its intrinsic content.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (HBR, based on *The Progress Principle*)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries finds the strongest predictor of inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Interesting-but-stalled work can feel worse than less glamorous work where you're visibly progressing.

Bottom line from the literature: your friends are overstating it — interest is one important factor among several (autonomy, growth, relationships, meaning, fit), and people reliably mispredict which factors will dominate their experience.

I need to cite real, well-known research on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, and meaningful work, while being careful not to invent papers. I should also acknowledge the research showing people often mispredicts what makes them happy, and include critiques of the "follow your passion" narrative. I'm finishing up the citation for that paper on implicit theories and passion, then noting some key counterpoints around leadership and autonomy. I want to make sure I'm presenting a balanced view—showing that while intrinsic motivation and fit matter, pay and management roles still play a role through autonomy and competence. I'm planning to stick to the user's request for just paper names and two-sentence summaries, keeping it to around 7-8 papers total.
rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
A meta-analysis of 92 studies found pay level correlates only ~0.15 with job satisfaction, meaning salary explains very little of how happy people are with their jobs. This supports the claim that pay differences (especially modest ones) are commonly overweighted in career decisions.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus at moderate income levels, while only abstract life evaluation keeps rising with income. A small raise on top of an already-high tech salary is unlikely to change how you actually feel day to day.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay ("job") or advancement ("career"). This is the strongest evidence for your friends' position—orientation toward the work itself predicts satisfaction better than rewards or promotion.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) – "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, and it predicts performance even when incentives are present. However, extrinsic incentives also matter independently—so the evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "interest matters almost exclusively."

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) – "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Notably, "meaningful" here includes work you find significant—but progress and good day-to-day conditions mattered as much as the topic itself.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) – "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain motivation across domains. This cuts against your friends: the OpenAI role might become interesting as you grow into it, especially the new challenge of leading a team.

**Chen, Ellsworth & Schwarz (2015) – "Finding a Fit or Developing It: Implicit Theories About Achieving Passion for Work"**
"Fit theorists" prioritize current enjoyment when choosing jobs but don't end up happier long-term than "develop theorists," who expect passion to grow with investment. Choosing purely on current interestingness is not clearly the winning strategy the "research shows."

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not interestingness alone. A leadership role you've always wanted may serve autonomy and competence needs in ways that staying as an IC, even on fascinating problems, does not.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) – "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting a single salient dimension (here, "interestingness" or "team lead title"). Whichever factor dominates your imagination right now is probably exerting more pull than it will exert on your actual future happiness.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are roughly half right—interest/intrinsic motivation is genuinely underrated relative to salary—but the evidence does not support weighting it "almost exclusively," especially against a long-held goal (leadership) that maps onto core psychological needs.

rows.answer:
**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"** — The foundational Job Characteristics Model shows that intrinsic motivation comes from five factors: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interesting work is only one input; autonomy and significance (which team leadership can provide) are equally weighted.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Sustained motivation and well-being depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A leadership role can boost autonomy and relatedness even if the task content is less stimulating, so "interestingness" alone doesn't determine intrinsic motivation.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only about r = .15 with job satisfaction. This supports partially discounting the salary difference, but it's evidence about pay being overrated, not interestingness being underrated.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts quality of performance, but extrinsic incentives independently predict quantity of output. The word "jointly" directly contradicts a "focus on interest almost exclusively" claim.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** — People systematically overestimate both the intensity and duration of their emotional reactions to future events (the "impact bias"). You're likely overestimating both how painful the "less interesting" work will feel and how thrilling the leadership role will be after the first few months.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** — Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning of their jobs, often turning mundane roles into engaging ones. The "interestingness" of a role is partly malleable, especially with the autonomy a team-lead position confers.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — People who see work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. This is the strongest support for your friends' position, though it's correlational and "calling" includes meaning, not just intellectual interest.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (and *The Progress Principle*)** — Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Notably, the driver is *progress and meaning*, not raw interestingness — a well-run team shipping things can score high here.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"** — This adversarial collaboration found emotional well-being does rise with income for most people, contrary to the earlier $75K plateau finding. A caveat against dismissing the salary bump entirely, though the effect size per dollar is modest at high incomes.

**Net of the literature**: interest/intrinsic motivation is genuinely important and pay is somewhat overrated, but no body of research supports weighting interestingness "almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, meaning, and relationships carry comparable weight, and several of those favor the leadership role.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** — Pay level correlates only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction, so the salary bump alone is a poor reason to switch. **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"** — Meta-analysis of 259 studies shows task variety, significance, and autonomy all predict satisfaction, but no single factor dominates exclusively. rows.reasoning: I'm thinking through which research papers would be most relevant here—specifically looking at foundational work on intrinsic motivation and how interesting work factors into employee engagement. I should stick to papers I'm confident are real, like Deci and Ryan's Self-Determination Theory work and Amabile's research on intrinsic motivation in the workplace. rows.answer:
**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) – "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation"**
A meta-analysis of 128 experiments showing that tangible rewards tend to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Supports your friends' general direction, but applies to rewards contingent on doing tasks, not career-level salary differences.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction. A modest salary bump like yours is unlikely to meaningfully move long-term satisfaction.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Supports weighting intrinsic interest heavily.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014) – "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Tracking 10,000+ cadets over a decade, internal motives predicted success, while holding strong instrumental motives (money, status) alongside them actually *worsened* outcomes. Caution: a 2020 replication attempt (using different data) found weaker effects.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) – "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Both intrinsic motivation and incentives predict performance—intrinsic motivation matters more for quality of work, incentives more for quantity. Directly contradicts the "almost exclusively" framing: it's both/and, not either/or.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) – "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The foundational Job Characteristics Model shows satisfaction is driven by skill variety, autonomy, task significance, identity, and feedback—not just task interestingness. Relevant because leading a team can increase autonomy and significance even if the technical content is duller.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth*—not interest alone. A leadership role you've always wanted may satisfy needs that interesting solo work doesn't.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) – "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those with a "growth theory" of interest develop passion for initially unappealing areas. The "less interesting" work might become interesting—and team leadership is a new domain you haven't yet developed interest in.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007) – "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"**
Meta-analysis of 259 studies finding motivational job characteristics explain a large share of variance in satisfaction, but social characteristics (interdependence, support) add substantial incremental prediction. Interesting work matters a lot; it is far from the only thing that does.

rows.answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong driver of well-being and performance, but it rests on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role offering growth and autonomy can satisfy these needs even if the task content is less fascinating.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation robustly predicts performance, especially quality of work, while incentives predict quantity. The key finding is "jointly" — intrinsic interest matters a lot, but the data don't support focusing on it exclusively.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Pay level correlates only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction across studies. This is the strongest evidence in your friends' favor: a "bit higher salary" predicts very little added satisfaction.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 per Year" (PNAS)**
Contrary to the famous Kahneman & Deaton plateau finding, experienced well-being continues rising with income with no clear satiation point. So money isn't irrelevant — though the effect is logarithmic, meaning a modest raise buys little.

**Brickman, Coates & Janoff-Bulman (1978), "Lottery Winners and Accident Victims: Is Happiness Relative?" (Journal of Personality and Social Psychology)**
The foundational hedonic adaptation study: people return toward baseline happiness after major financial windfalls. Salary bumps tend to fade from daily experience faster than the lived texture of your day-to-day work.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Cadets with strong internal motives had better long-term outcomes, and adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually weakened this effect. Suggests taking a job "for the title and salary" can dilute the motivational benefits of the work itself.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when work gets hard, while those who believe interests develop sustain engagement. Relevant caveat: the OpenAI role being "less interesting" now may not be a fixed property — interest in leading a team can grow.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work: A Meta-Analysis" (Personnel Psychology)**
Person–job and person–organization fit predict satisfaction, commitment, and retention more strongly than any single job attribute. Fit includes alignment with your goals (e.g., your long-held desire to lead), not just task interest.

**Bottom line:** the research strongly supports your friends' direction — interest and intrinsic motivation predict satisfaction far better than modest pay differences — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and goal-fit (like finally leading a team) carry comparable weight.

rows.answer:
**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Across ~200,000 participants, intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, but extrinsic incentives also mattered and the two coexist. This supports interest being important, but not that it should be weighted "almost exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — Pay level correlates with job satisfaction only weakly (r ≈ .15), suggesting people overestimate how much a raise will improve how they feel about work. This is one of the strongest data points for your friends' position.

**Hsee & Hastie (2006), "Decision and Experience: Why Don't We Choose What Makes Us Happy?" (Trends in Cognitive Sciences)** — People systematically overweight quantifiable, comparable attributes (like salary) and underweight experiential ones (like how engaging the day-to-day work is) when choosing between options. Directly supports the claim that interestingness is underrated *at decision time*.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)** — Cadets with strong internal motives had better long-term outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (status, money) actually predicted worse outcomes. Suggests taking a job mainly for salary/title can dilute the motivational benefit of the work itself.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features" (Journal of Applied Psychology)** — Meta-analysis showing job satisfaction is driven by a *bundle* of features: autonomy, task variety, significance, feedback, and social factors — not interestingness alone. Notably, leading a team can increase autonomy and significance, which complicates a simple "interesting > everything" rule.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)** — People who believe interests are developed (rather than fixed and "found") sustain engagement better when work gets hard or initially seems dull. Relevant caveat: work that seems "much less interesting" upfront may become interesting, especially with new challenges like leadership.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS)** — Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves life evaluation but not day-to-day emotional experience. At a Meta/Google compensation level, a "bit higher salary" is unlikely to move daily well-being much. (See Killingsworth 2021, PNAS, for a partial counterpoint showing continued log-linear gains.)

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019), "Promotions and the Peter Principle" (Quarterly Journal of Economics)** — Strong individual contributors are often promoted into management despite managerial skill being poorly predicted by IC performance, and the transition frequently disappoints. Worth weighing against the genuine value of testing your long-held desire to lead.

Bottom line from the literature: your friends are directionally right (people do underweight intrinsic interest and overweight salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, and trying leadership are also well-supported drivers of satisfaction.

rows.answer:
**Evidence supporting your friends' view:**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction, meaning salary explains a tiny fraction of how happy people are at work. This suggests a "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014) — "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Tracking over 10,000 cadets, intrinsic motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (money, status) not only failed to help but actually *undermined* outcomes when held alongside intrinsic ones. A key citation for the "interest is underrated" camp.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Decades of evidence show sustained motivation and well-being depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness — with intrinsic interest being central. Note the complication for you: leading a team could *increase* autonomy and relatedness, even if the topic is duller.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on *meaningful* work. Meaningfulness here includes interest, but also impact and ownership — which a leadership role can provide.

**Evidence complicating your friends' view:**

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are "fixed" give up when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are *developed* grow passionate about new domains over time. Implication: the Google work might become interesting once you invest in it — interest is partly endogenous.

**Cech (2021) — "The Trouble with Passion: How Searching for Fulfillment at Work Fosters Inequality" (book, UC Press)**
Sociological evidence that the "follow your passion" principle leads people to discount stability, pay, and structural factors in ways they later regret. A direct academic critique of treating interestingness as almost the only criterion.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting the salient feature of a choice (here: "less interesting") and underestimating adaptation. Your forecast that the Google role will feel boring is itself unreliable — in both directions.

**Bottom line from the literature:** intrinsic interest matters more than salary differences and is genuinely underweighted by most people — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and meaning (which team leadership you've "always wanted" may supply) are equally robust predictors, and interest itself can develop in new roles.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction, meaning salary differences explain very little of how happy people are at work. This supports discounting the modest pay bump in your decision.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong, consistent predictor of performance quality across hundreds of studies. However, it operates *jointly* with extrinsic incentives rather than exclusively—which cuts against your friends' "almost exclusively" framing.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets" (PNAS)**
Tracking 10,000+ cadets over a decade, internal motives (genuine interest) predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (pay, status) actually predicted worse outcomes—even when combined with internal motives. One of the strongest empirical cases for prioritizing interest.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness needs that an individual-contributor role may not, complicating a pure "interestingness" calculus.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Job satisfaction is driven by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. A team-lead role may score higher on autonomy and significance even if lower on intrinsic task interest.

People systematically overestimate how much both positive and negative job changes will affect their happiness, and adapt faster than predicted. Relevant warning: you may adapt to "less interesting" work faster than you fear—and also to the status of leading a team faster than you hope.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning, often turning initially unengaging roles into satisfying ones. As a team lead, you'd likely have *more* latitude to craft work toward your interests over time.

**Bottom line the literature suggests:** your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest is underweighted relative to pay—but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and relational needs (which the leadership role offers) are comparably strong predictors of long-term satisfaction.

Quick note before the list: I'm made by Anthropic, so factor in that potential conflict of interest when weighing anything I say about this particular choice. Now, the papers:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Across 115 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. This supports your friends' implicit claim that salary differences matter less than people assume.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity. Suggests interesting work doesn't just feel better — you'll likely do better work.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness alone. Notably, leading a team could serve autonomy and relatedness needs that "interesting IC work" might not.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
The foundational job characteristics model identifies five drivers of motivation: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interestingness is roughly one-to-two of these five dimensions, which cuts against the "almost exclusively" framing.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those with a "growth theory" of interest develop passion for initially less-interesting work. This suggests the new role's interestingness is partly something you'd construct, not a fixed property.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Employees actively reshape task boundaries and meaning of their roles, often substantially changing how engaging the work is. A team-lead role may give you more leverage to craft the work toward what interests you.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events, including job changes. Both your excitement about "interesting" and dread about "less interesting" are probably overestimated in durability.

**Nieß & Zacher (2015), "Openness to Experience as a Predictor and Outcome of Upward Job Changes into Managerial and Professional Positions"**
Moves into managerial roles relate to personality and developmental outcomes, not just current preferences. Relevant because your stated long-term desire to lead a team is itself a data point the "interestingness-only" view ignores.

**Bottom line:** the literature supports "interestingness is underrated relative to pay," but does *not* support "focus on it almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, and long-held goals (like wanting to lead) carry comparable weight.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior***
A meta-analysis of 115 studies found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This supports your friends' general direction: a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about your job.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being," *American Psychologist***
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team for the first time could satisfy autonomy and competence needs, which complicates the "interestingness only" framing.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality***
People who view their work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. This is the strongest evidence for your friends' side — but note that for some people, advancement and leadership *is* part of what makes work feel like a calling.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. This contradicts the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" *Psychological Science***
People who believe passions are "found" (fixed) lose interest when work gets hard, while those who believe interest can be developed sustain engagement in new areas. Relevant caveat for you: the "less interesting" OpenAI role might become interesting once you're invested in it — especially the management challenge you say you've always wanted.

**Nicholson & De Waal-Andrews (2005) / related literature on regret — see Gilovich & Medvec (1995), "The experience of regret: What, when, and why," *Psychological Review***
People's most enduring regrets concern inactions and unfulfilled aspirations rather than failed attempts. If team leadership is something you've "always wanted," that's a regret-relevant data point that pure interestingness comparisons miss.

**Bottom line from the literature:** Your friends are directionally right (interest beats salary as a satisfaction predictor) but wrong on "almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, and fulfilling long-held aspirations are also robust predictors, and your OpenAI offer happens to contain exactly those.

**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity" (JPSP).** People systematically underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter when choosing future activities, but once actually doing the work, enjoyment dominates persistence and satisfaction. This is the strongest direct support for your friends' claim that "interestingness" is underrated at decision time.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior).** Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only about r = .15 with job satisfaction and r = .23 with pay satisfaction itself. A modestly higher salary is a weak predictor of being happier in a role.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Self-Determination Theory" (Psychological Inquiry).** Well-being at work depends on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can serve these needs (especially autonomy and growth), so the Meta offer isn't purely an "extrinsic" option.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin).** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts the quality of work performance, but extrinsic incentives matter too, particularly for quantity-based outcomes. The evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "focus on it almost exclusively."

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance).** Job satisfaction is driven by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interestingness maps mainly onto one or two of these; a leadership role can score higher on autonomy and significance even if the content is duller.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review).** Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making daily progress on meaningful work. This favors whichever job offers clearer progress and meaning to you — which may or may not be the "more interesting" one.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review).** People substantially reshape how interesting and meaningful their jobs are after taking them. This cuts against treating "interestingness as advertised" as a fixed property of either offer.

**Gilbert & Wilson (2000), "Miswanting: Some Problems in the Forecasting of Future Affective States" (in *Feeling and Thinking*).** People reliably mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like a title or a prestigious project) and underweighting daily texture. A caution applicable to both options — and to your friends' confidence in a single decision rule.

Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives matter more for quantity-based outcomes. Importantly, the two are not mutually exclusive—both contribute jointly.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Argues sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth*—not interest alone. A leadership role you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that "interesting work" doesn't cover.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
Shows that people who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement. Implies the Google role might *become* interesting—interest isn't a static property of the job.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005), "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect" (Journal of Applied Psychology)**
Job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that fades within about a year as satisfaction reverts toward baseline. A caution against expecting any single move to permanently change how you feel.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who see their work as a "calling" report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Notably, orientation depends partly on the person and how they frame the work, not just objective job features.

---

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — A meta-analysis of 92 samples found pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15, meaning salary explains very little of how happy people are at work. This supports the idea that a modest pay bump should carry little weight in your decision.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" (American Psychologist)** — Foundational work showing that intrinsic motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and growth needs, so "interestingness" is not the only intrinsic factor in play.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)** — Tracking over 10,000 cadets, internal motives predicted long-term success while instrumental motives (money, status) predicted worse outcomes, even when held alongside internal ones. Partial support for your friends: optimizing for external rewards can actively backfire.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Across 183 studies, intrinsic motivation was a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives still mattered, especially for quantity-based outcomes. This undercuts the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim — the evidence supports "weighted heavily," not "exclusive."

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" (Psychological Science)** — People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly, while those with a "growth" view develop deep interest in initially unappealing areas. Relevant caveat: the Google role might become interesting once you're invested, especially the management challenge.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work" (Journal of Research in Personality)** — People who see work as a "calling" report significantly higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. However, calling orientation comes partly from how you frame the work, not just from the work itself.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High income improves evaluation of life but not emotional well-being" (PNAS)** — Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how you *evaluate* your life on reflection but not your day-to-day emotional experience. At Meta/Google compensation levels, you are almost certainly past the point where the salary difference affects daily happiness.

**Bottom line in two sentences:** The research broadly supports your friends' direction — interest and intrinsic motivation are underweighted relative to pay — but not the "almost exclusively" framing, since autonomy, growth, and leadership opportunity are themselves major intrinsic-motivation drivers. The Google offer's team-lead component is itself an intrinsic factor, so this isn't a clean "interesting vs. boring" tradeoff.
