rows.answer:
**Papers supporting your friends' claim:**

**Hsee, Zhang, Yu & Xi (2003), "Lay Rationalism and Inconsistency Between Predicted Experience and Decision"** — People systematically overweight "hard" attributes like salary and title when choosing, even when they predict that "soft" experiential attributes will matter more for their actual happiness. The authors call this "lay rationalism" and show it leads to choices people themselves expect to enjoy less.

**Woolley & Fishbach (2015), "The Experience Matters More Than You Think: People Value Intrinsic Incentives More Inside Than Outside an Activity"** — When choosing activities in advance, people underweight how much intrinsic enjoyment will matter to them once they're actually doing the work. This suggests day-to-day interestingness is systematically underrated at decision time.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction. A modest salary bump is unlikely to move overall satisfaction much.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work, while incentives predict quantity more. For research-type work, finding it interesting likely makes you better at it.

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — People who believe interests are fixed give up when work gets hard, while those with a growth theory develop interest in new domains. What seems "much less interesting" now may become interesting once you're immersed — especially in a new role like leading a team.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"** — Well-being at work depends on autonomy, competence, and relatedness, not interestingness alone. A leadership role you've long wanted could satisfy these needs in ways an individually interesting project doesn't.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"** — People overestimate the intensity and duration of future emotional reactions and underestimate adaptation. This cuts both ways: you'd adapt to the higher salary quickly, but possibly also to the loss of interestingness.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** — People who see their work as a calling report substantially higher work and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Notably, orientation depends partly on how you relate to the work, not just the work itself.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right — interest is underweighted relative to pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; fulfilled long-term aspirations (like leading a team) and need satisfaction matter too, and interest itself can grow in a new role.

rows.reasoning: I need to be careful not to invent papers, so I'm thinking through actual research on intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction. Judge, Thoresen, Bono & Patton's 2001 meta-analysis on the job satisfaction-performance relationship is a solid starting point, and I should look for other well-established studies on whether meaningful work matters more than compensation or management factors. rows.answer:
**Papers broadly supporting your friends' claim:**

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Harvard Business Review)**
Herzberg's two-factor theory argues that "hygiene factors" like salary prevent dissatisfaction but don't create satisfaction, while "motivators" like interesting work and achievement drive genuine engagement. This is the classic argument that pay differences matter less than the nature of the work itself.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
This meta-analysis found pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only about r = .15 — surprisingly weak. People at vastly different salary levels report similar satisfaction with their jobs.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Across 128 experiments, tangible extrinsic rewards tended to undermine intrinsic motivation for interesting tasks. Suggests that taking a job primarily for money can erode the enjoyment you'd otherwise get from the work.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who view their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. The effect held even within the same occupation.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analyzing ~12,000 diary entries from knowledge workers, the strongest predictor of positive inner work life was making progress on meaningful work. Notably, even managers vastly underestimated this factor relative to incentives.

**Papers complicating the claim:**

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved" (PNAS)**
This adversarial collaboration found that happiness does continue rising with income well beyond $75k for most people, overturning the famous earlier plateau finding. Money isn't nothing — though the marginal effect of a modest raise at already-high salaries is small.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness per se. Relevant because leading a team (something you've always wanted) could satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the topic is duller.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction is predicted by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — interesting content is only part of the picture. A leadership role can score high on autonomy and significance even with less interesting subject matter.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features and underweighting adaptation. Cuts both ways here: you may adapt to the boredom of less interesting work — or the thrill of leading a team may fade faster than you expect.

**Benson, Li & Shue (2019), "Promotions and the Peter Principle" (Quarterly Journal of Economics)**
Using data on sales workers, the best individual contributors who were promoted into management often performed worse as managers, since IC skill doesn't predict management skill. Worth weighing whether you want to lead a team because you'll be good at and enjoy it, or because it's a status marker.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interesting work and intrinsic motivation are underrated relative to pay — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since autonomy, growth, and fit with long-held goals (like leading a team) are comparably strong predictors of satisfaction.

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior***
Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. This supports your friends' direction: a modest salary bump is unlikely to meaningfully change how you feel about work.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets," *PNAS***
West Point cadets with strong internal motives were more likely to graduate and thrive in their careers, while instrumental motives (money, status) predicted worse outcomes even when held alongside internal ones. Often cited as evidence that adding extrinsic reasons to an intrinsically motivating path can backfire.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently predict performance too, especially quantity-based outcomes. This complicates the "almost exclusively" claim — both matter, they just matter for different things.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "The 'what' and 'why' of goal pursuits: Human needs and the self-determination of behavior," *Psychological Inquiry***
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not just task interest. Relevant caveat for you: leading a team (a long-held goal) could satisfy autonomy and growth needs that "interesting work" alone doesn't.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" *Psychological Science***
People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when new domains get hard; those with a "developed" mindset cultivate interest in initially less-exciting areas. Suggests the Google role might become more interesting than it currently looks — and that management itself can become a passion.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced well-being rises with income, even above $75,000 per year," *PNAS***
Contradicting the famous Kahneman & Deaton (2010) plateau finding, this large experience-sampling study found well-being continues rising with income with no clear satiation point. The effect is real but logarithmic — small relative raises buy little happiness.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature"** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15, meaning salary explains very little of how happy people are at work. This supports your friends' general direction: once pay is adequate, marginal increases buy surprisingly little satisfaction.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis"** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong, consistent predictor of performance, especially for quality of work (vs. quantity). However, it found extrinsic incentives also matter and the two operate jointly — undermining the "almost exclusively" part of the claim.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being"** — Long-term motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Notably, a leadership role can satisfy autonomy and competence needs that an individual-contributor role may not, which complicates a pure "interestingness" framing.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?"** — People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard; those who believe interests develop sustain engagement across domains. Relevant to you because the "much less interesting" team-lead work may become interesting as you develop competence in it — interest is partly endogenous to investment.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work"** — People who frame work as a "calling" report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those framing it as a job (money) or career (advancement). Worth asking which offer better matches your sense of calling — note that "leading a team, which I always wanted" might itself be the calling signal here.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want"** — People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like a salary bump or a title) and underweighting daily texture of life. A caution against both your friends' confident claim *and* your own predictions about which job will feel better.

**Verdict in one line:** the research supports "intrinsic interest is heavily underrated relative to pay," but not "focus on it almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, and fulfilled long-held aspirations (like leading a team) are comparably powerful predictors.

*Disclosure: I'm made by Anthropic, so weigh my framing accordingly — though I've tried to let the literature speak for itself.*

rows.answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist).** Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts engagement and wellbeing, but it rests on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness alone. Notably, a leadership role can serve autonomy and competence needs, so "interesting" isn't the only path to intrinsic satisfaction.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior).** Across studies, pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. This supports your friends' claim insofar as a moderate salary bump is a weak predictor of how happy you'll actually be.

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson (2005), "Consequences of Individuals' Fit at Work" (Personnel Psychology).** This meta-analysis finds person-job fit — alignment between your needs/abilities and the job — is one of the strongest predictors of satisfaction and commitment. "Fit" includes growth needs like wanting to lead a team, not just topic interest.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality).** People who experience work as a calling report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Supports weighting meaning/interest heavily — though "calling" is about purpose, which can come from impact or leadership too.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review).** Analysis of ~12,000 work diary entries found the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Interesting work helps mainly when it also lets you make visible progress.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science).** People systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future choices, and adapt to changes (like raises) faster than expected. This cuts both ways: you'd likely adapt to the higher salary, but you may also be misjudging how much the leadership opportunity would mean to you.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved" (PNAS).** This adversarial collaboration found wellbeing does rise with income for most people, contradicting the popular "$75k plateau" claim. So money isn't irrelevant — it's just a weaker lever than fit and intrinsic factors.

**Bottom line:** the research supports weighting interest heavily, but not "almost exclusively" — fit, autonomy, growth (e.g., your team-leading ambition), and to a lesser degree pay all carry real weight.

rows.answer:
**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Foundational paper showing that intrinsic job characteristics (skill variety, task significance, autonomy, feedback) strongly predict motivation and satisfaction. Supports your friends' view that the nature of the work itself matters a great deal.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007), "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"**
Meta-analysis of 259 studies confirming motivational characteristics matter, but finding that social characteristics (support, interdependence, feedback from others) explain substantial *incremental* variance in satisfaction and turnover. Undercuts the "almost exclusively" part of your friends' claim.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Found pay level correlates only about .15 with job satisfaction—people earning very different salaries report similar satisfaction. Suggests the "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Argues sustained motivation requires three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness*—not interestingness alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if task content is less stimulating.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic factors independently matter and don't simply crowd it out. Suggests a "both/and" framing rather than focusing on one factor exclusively.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People with a "calling" orientation report the highest work and life satisfaction, regardless of occupation prestige. Relevant nuance: whether work feels meaningful depends partly on your orientation toward it, not just objective task content.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
Shows people actively reshape task boundaries and relationships to make jobs more engaging over time. Implies a "less interesting" role—especially one you lead—may be more malleable than it looks from the outside.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overweighting salient features (like "this is boring") and underestimating adaptation. A caution against confidently predicting your happiness in *either* role from your current vantage point.

**Verdict from the literature:** Your friends are directionally right that intrinsic interest beats salary, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, relationships, growth, and meaning (which team leadership can provide) are comparably powerful predictors.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view their work as a "calling" (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and job satisfaction than those with "job" (money) or "career" (advancement) orientations. Notably, the "career" orientation—your Google offer's leadership appeal—scored lowest on satisfaction in some comparisons, though orientation is partly about mindset, not just the role itself.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15—remarkably weak. This suggests a modest salary bump is unlikely to move the needle much on how you feel day to day.

**Brickman, Coates & Janoff-Bulman (1978), "Lottery Winners and Accident Victims: Is Happiness Relative?"**
Even lottery winners adapted back toward baseline happiness, while deriving less pleasure from ordinary daily activities. Classic evidence for hedonic adaptation: salary increases fade fast, while the texture of daily work persists.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being and sustained motivation depend on autonomy, competence, and relatedness—and intrinsically interesting work feeds these directly. Caveat for your case: leading a team can also satisfy autonomy and competence needs, so "interesting" isn't the only path to need fulfillment.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback jointly drive internal motivation and satisfaction—"interestingness" is only one of several core job characteristics. A team-lead role could score higher on autonomy and significance even if lower on intrinsic interest.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong, robust predictor of performance quality, while incentives predict quantity more than quality. Supports your friends' view that interest matters greatly—but "jointly predict" is the operative phrase; it's not exclusive.

**Johnston & Lee (2013), "Extra Status and Extra Stress: Are Promotions Good for Us?"**
Using longitudinal data, promotions improved perceived job security and satisfaction briefly, but mental health worsened in subsequent years with no lasting satisfaction gain. A cautionary note on assuming the leadership role itself will deliver durable happiness.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want"**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overestimating both the joy of gains and the pain of losses. Cuts both ways here: you may be overweighting the appeal of leading a team *and* the dread of boring work.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Interesting work where you make visible progress is hard to beat; managing without progress on things you care about can be draining.

**Bottom line embedded in the literature:** your friends are directionally right—interest and intrinsic motivation predict satisfaction far better than pay does—but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth, and long-held goals (like leading a team) are legitimate, evidence-backed inputs too.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist***
Argues that well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can serve autonomy and competence needs, which complicates a "pick the interesting job" heuristic.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior***
Finds pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15 across 86 studies. Supports your friends' point that a modest salary bump is a weak predictor of how you'll actually feel at work.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***
Shows intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, while extrinsic incentives matter more for quantity-based outcomes. However, both jointly predict performance—it's not "interest exclusively."

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets," *PNAS***
Found that cadets with strong internal motives had better long-term career outcomes, and adding instrumental motives (pay, status) actually *weakened* outcomes. One of the strongest empirical cases for prioritizing intrinsic interest.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality***
People who view work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Caveat: the calling orientation is about meaning, which isn't identical to "interestingness."

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" *Psychological Science***
Shows interests are often developed rather than fixed and discovered, and people with a "fixed" theory of interest disengage faster when work gets hard. A counterpoint: the Google role might *become* interesting, especially given your stated desire to lead.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want," *Current Directions in Psychological Science***
Demonstrates that people systematically mispredict the intensity and duration of their future feelings about decisions. A caution against confident predictions—yours or your friends'—about which job will make you happier.

**Nikolova & Cnossen (2020), "What Makes Work Meaningful and Why Economists Should Care About It," *Labour Economics***
Using European survey data, finds autonomy, competence, and relatedness explain far more variance in meaningful work than income or benefits. Again suggests interest matters more than pay—but so do growth opportunities like the leadership role you've always wanted.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (interest/intrinsic motivation beats marginal salary), but "almost exclusively" is an overstatement—autonomy, growth, and leadership opportunities are comparably strong predictors, and your interests may be more malleable than you think.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being," *American Psychologist***
Decades of evidence show well-being and sustained motivation depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness—not just intrinsic interest in tasks. Notably, leading a team can serve these needs too, so "interestingness" isn't the only channel to fulfillment.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis," *Psychological Bulletin***

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins," *Harvard Business Review* (based on *The Progress Principle*)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the strongest driver of positive inner work life is making daily progress in meaningful work, more than recognition or pay. **Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality***

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting," *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology***

**Verdict the literature actually supports:** interest is underrated relative to salary, but "almost exclusively" overstates it—autonomy, progress, growth, and relationships (all things a leadership role can provide) matter comparably.

**Kahneman, D., Krueger, A. B., Schkade, D., Schwarz, N., & Stone, A. A. (2006). "Would You Be Happier If You Were Richer? A Focusing Illusion." *Science*.**
Shows that people drastically overestimate how much income improvements affect day-to-day happiness because the salary difference is salient at decision time but fades from attention afterward. Supports the idea that the pay bump will matter less than it feels like it will right now.

**Judge, T. A., Piccolo, R. F., Podsakoff, N. P., Shaw, J. C., & Rich, B. L. (2010). "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis." *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
A meta-analysis of 86 studies finding that pay level correlates only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. Suggests a "bit higher salary" is a poor predictor of how you'll actually feel about the job.

**Wrzesniewski, A., McCauley, C., Rozin, P., & Schwartz, B. (1997). "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work." *Journal of Research in Personality*.**
People who view work as intrinsically meaningful ("callings") report higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Partial support for your friends' claim.

**Ryan, R. M., & Deci, E. L. (2000). "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being." *American Psychologist*.**
Sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and *relatedness/growth* — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team you've always wanted to lead could satisfy autonomy and competence needs, complicating a pure "interestingness" rule.

**Cerasoli, C. P., Nicklin, J. M., & Ford, M. T. (2014). "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis." *Psychological Bulletin*.**
Intrinsic motivation is the stronger predictor of work quality, but extrinsic incentives still independently predict performance, especially for quantity-based outcomes. Evidence against the "almost exclusively" framing — both channels matter.

**Amabile, T. M., & Kramer, S. J. (2011). *The Progress Principle* (and the underlying diary study, "The Power of Small Wins," *HBR*).**
Analysis of ~12,000 daily work diaries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on *meaningful* work, more than recognition or incentives. Meaning and progress, not raw "interestingness," are the active ingredients — a role with more ownership can score highly here.

**Wilson, T. D., & Gilbert, D. T. (2005). "Affective Forecasting: Knowing What to Want." *Current Directions in Psychological Science*.**
People are systematically bad at predicting how future circumstances will make them feel, both overestimating the impact of single salient factors and underestimating adaptation. A caution against confidently forecasting that *either* the interesting work or the team-lead role will deliver as imagined.

**Hsee, C. K., & Hastie, R. (2006). "Decision and Experience: Why Don't We Choose What Makes Us Happy?" *Trends in Cognitive Sciences*.**
People predictably choose options that score well on quantifiable, justifiable dimensions (salary, title) over options that score well on experiential ones (daily engagement). The closest direct support for your friends' claim that experienced interestingness is systematically underweighted in decisions.

**Duffy, R. D., & Dik, B. J. (2013). "Research on Calling: What Have We Learned and Where Are We Going?" *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.**
A review noting that pursuing a calling has benefits but also documented downsides, including exploitation, burnout, and tunnel vision. A counterweight to making interestingness an *exclusive* criterion.

**Net of the literature:** your friends are directionally right — experienced engagement is underweighted relative to salary — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; the unmet desire to lead maps onto autonomy/growth needs that the same literature says matter a lot.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"**
Meta-analysis showing interest–job fit reliably predicts performance and persistence, but with moderate effect sizes (~.20–.30). Interest matters, but not enough to justify "almost exclusively."

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of work quality, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity, and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. Supports weighting interest heavily, but explicitly rejects an "exclusively" framing.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). Strong evidence that the "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income, well below either of your likely offers. The marginal salary difference is probably hedonically irrelevant for you.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. A leadership role you've "always wanted" may satisfy autonomy and growth needs that interesting-but-IC work doesn't.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People with a "fixed" theory of interest assume passion is found in the right task and disengage when work gets hard; interest can in fact be developed through engagement. Cuts against treating the current interestingness of each role as a fixed property.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
People substantially reshape how meaningful and interesting their work is through job crafting. Suggests a "less interesting" role with more authority may give you more leverage to make the work interesting.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins"**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the strongest driver of motivation and positive inner work life is making progress in meaningful work. Whichever role gives you clearer progress and meaning will likely dominate day-to-day satisfaction.

---

**Bottom line:** Your friends are half right. Research supports weighting intrinsic interest heavily and salary lightly — but "almost exclusively" overstates it: autonomy, growth, and pursuing a long-held goal (like leading a team) are independently strong predictors of satisfaction, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.

papers.paper: Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"
papers.description: Meta-analysis showing interest–job fit reliably predicts performance and persistence, but with moderate effect sizes (~.20–.30). Interest matters, but not enough to justify "almost exclusively."

papers.paper: Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"
papers.description: Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of work quality, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity, and the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. Supports weighting interest heavily, but explicitly rejects an "exclusively" framing.

papers.paper: Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"
papers.description: Pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). Strong evidence that the "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision.

papers.paper: Kahneman & Deaton (2010) — "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"
papers.description: Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus around a moderate income, well below either of your likely offers. The marginal salary difference is probably hedonically irrelevant for you.

papers.paper: Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"
papers.description: Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. A leadership role you've "always wanted" may satisfy autonomy and growth needs that interesting-but-IC work doesn't.

papers.paper: O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"
papers.description: People with a "fixed" theory of interest assume passion is found in the right task and disengage when work gets hard; interest can in fact be developed through engagement. Cuts against treating the current interestingness of each role as a fixed property.

papers.paper: Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"
papers.description: People substantially reshape how meaningful and interesting their work is through job crafting. Suggests a "less interesting" role with more authority may give you more leverage to make the work interesting.

papers.paper: Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The Power of Small Wins"
papers.description: Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the strongest driver of motivation and positive inner work life is making progress in meaningful work. Whichever role gives you clearer progress and meaning will likely dominate day-to-day satisfaction.

rows.reasoning: I need to find real, verifiable research papers on intrinsic motivation and job satisfaction to answer whether interesting work is genuinely underrated. Pay shows only a weak correlation with job satisfaction overall. So the research points to a nuanced picture: interest is genuinely important, but saying it's the *only* factor misses the complexity. I'll keep each summary tight—two sentences max.
rows.answer:
**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being," *American Psychologist*.** Argues that well-being depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — intrinsic interest matters greatly but is not the only driver. Notably, leading a team can serve the competence and relatedness needs, complicating an "interest-only" rule.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis," *Psychological Bulletin*.** Finds intrinsic motivation is a medium-to-strong predictor of performance, especially for quality of work. However, intrinsic and extrinsic factors predict outcomes *jointly* — the data do not support focusing on interest "almost exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*.** Pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15) across studies. This supports your friends' general direction: a "bit higher salary" is unlikely to move your satisfaction much.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" *Psychological Science*.** Shows that people who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly from new domains, while interests can in fact be cultivated through engagement. Relevant because management — something you "always wanted" — could become genuinely interesting rather than remaining "much less interesting."

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality*.** People who view their work as a calling report substantially higher life and job satisfaction than those with job or career orientations. Orientation depends partly on the person and how they frame the work, not only on the work's objective content.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005), "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect," *Journal of Applied Psychology*.** Job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that fades within about a year as the new role's downsides surface. A caution against weighting the novel appeal of the offer (title, team lead) too heavily.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting," *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*.** People systematically overestimate how intensely and how long future events — including job changes — will affect their happiness. Both "I'll be bored at the less interesting job" and "leading a team will fulfill me" are forecasts likely to be exaggerated.

**Killingsworth (2021), "Experienced Well-Being Rises with Income, Even Above $75,000 Per Year," *PNAS*.** Contradicts the popular claim that money stops mattering past a threshold, finding well-being rises log-linearly with income. Included for balance: the salary difference isn't *zero* information, just log-scaled.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (interest beats marginal pay) but wrong on "almost exclusively" — autonomy, growth, and whether you'd find leadership intrinsically rewarding matter comparably.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)** — Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, while extrinsic incentives better predict quantity of output. Importantly, the two *jointly* predict performance, so the evidence supports "interest matters a lot," not "focus on it exclusively."

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. This is the strongest evidence for your friends' position: marginal salary differences predict surprisingly little about how you'll feel at work.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)** — Well-being at work depends on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Note this cuts both ways for you: interesting work feeds these needs, but so can leading a team (autonomy, growth into a desired role).

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work" (Journal of Research in Personality)** — People who see work as a "calling" report substantially higher life and work satisfaction than those oriented toward pay ("job") or advancement ("career"). However, the calling orientation depends on perceived meaning, which can come from leading people as much as from intellectually interesting problems.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being" (PNAS)** — Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaus with income at a fairly modest level, suggesting marginal salary gains at your likely compensation buy little happiness. **Sheldon & Lyubomirsky (2006), "Achieving Sustainable Gains in Happiness: Change Your Actions, Not Your Circumstances" (Journal of Happiness Studies)** — People hedonically adapt quickly to circumstantial changes (like salary) but much more slowly to changes in engaging activities. This favors weighting the day-to-day texture of the work heavily over compensation.

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Harvard Business Review)** — Two-factor theory: hygiene factors (pay, conditions) prevent dissatisfaction but don't create satisfaction, while motivators (interesting work, responsibility, growth) drive genuine engagement. Notably, "responsibility and advancement" — i.e., leading a team — are classified as motivators, not hygiene factors.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right that interestingness beats salary, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — and the research classifies *leadership responsibility and growth* alongside interesting work as an intrinsic motivator, so this may be less of an intrinsic-vs-extrinsic tradeoff than it looks.

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) – "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 92 samples, pay level correlated only ~.15 with job satisfaction, suggesting salary differences (especially modest ones) buy surprisingly little happiness. This supports your friends' general direction, though it says nothing about ignoring other factors.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) – "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being and sustained motivation depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interestingness alone. Notably, a leadership role you've always wanted could satisfy autonomy and competence needs, complicating the "interest only" advice.

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson (2007) – "Integrating Motivational, Social, and Contextual Work Design Features: A Meta-Analytic Summary"**
This meta-analysis of 259 studies found task variety, significance, and autonomy strongly predict satisfaction, but social features (support, feedback, interdependence) explain substantial additional variance. Interesting work matters a lot; it just isn't the whole picture.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) – "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"**
People who believe interests are fixed give up faster when work gets hard; those with a "growth" theory of interest develop passion in new domains. This directly undercuts the idea that you should treat current interest as a fixed constraint — you might come to find team leadership fascinating.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) – "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who see their work as a calling report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job or career orientations, even within the same occupation. Orientation toward work matters as much as the work's objective content.

**Vallerand et al. (2003) – "Les Passions de l'Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion"**
Passion for work predicts well-being only when it's "harmonious" (integrated with life) rather than "obsessive." Pursuing interestingness "almost exclusively" risks the obsessive pattern, which correlates with worse outcomes.

**Cech (2021) – *The Trouble with Passion* (book)**
Sociological research showing the "passion principle" — choose work for fulfillment above all — often leads people to accept worse conditions and underweight structural factors like career capital. A useful counterweight to your friends' claim.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010) – "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Beyond a comfortable threshold, additional income improves how people evaluate their lives on reflection but not their day-to-day emotional experience. Since you're likely well past that threshold, the salary delta probably matters less than either daily interest or the leadership opportunity.

**Bottom line from the literature:** interest is genuinely important and pay is overrated, but "almost exclusively" overstates it — autonomy, growth, fit, and whether leadership fulfills a long-held need all carry real empirical weight.

The user's friends have a point—intrinsic motivation and meaningful work do matter significantly—but the claim that it should be pursued "almost exclusively" oversimplifies what the literature shows. I need to find papers on intrinsic motivation, job satisfaction, meaning at work, and related topics, while being careful to represent what the research actually supports rather than just confirming their hypothesis. Continuing with more foundational research on motivation and decision-making — papers on how intrinsic motivation predicts performance quality, how money matters but only up to a point, the Job Characteristics Model's framework for meaningful work, and research showing people often don't choose based purely on happiness but other values too.

I'm thinking through how to structure this: the research actually supports that intrinsic interest matters and pay weakly predicts satisfaction, but saying motivation is "almost exclusively" intrinsic overstates it — motivation can be developed, and leadership and growth opportunities play real roles. I'll organize the papers into two groups — those supporting the intrinsic motivation view and those that complicate it — with just the paper names and brief summaries as requested.
rows.answer:
**Papers broadly supporting your friends' claim:**

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010). "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature." *Journal of Vocational Behavior.***
A meta-analysis of 115 studies found the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). People earning very different amounts report similar satisfaction levels, suggesting pay differences alone are a poor basis for job choices.

**Deci & Ryan (2000). "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being." *American Psychologist.***
Foundational work showing that wellbeing and sustained motivation depend on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness, which intrinsically interesting work tends to provide. Extrinsic rewards can even undermine intrinsic motivation under certain conditions.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014). "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets." *PNAS.***
A study of over 10,000 cadets found that internal motives predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (status, money) actually *weakened* outcomes even when internal motives were also present. Suggests choosing primarily for external rewards may be actively counterproductive.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011). "The power of small wins." *Harvard Business Review.***
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress on meaningful work. Day-to-day engagement with the work itself outweighed recognition and incentives.

**Papers complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014). "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis." *Psychological Bulletin.***
Intrinsic motivation strongly predicts performance quality, but extrinsic incentives independently matter too, especially for quantity-based outcomes. The two are not mutually exclusive, contradicting an "exclusive" focus on interest.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018). "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" *Psychological Science.***
Interests are not fixed; people who believe passions are "found" give up faster when work gets hard, while interests can be actively developed in new domains. Relevant because a "less interesting" role (e.g., team leadership) may become interesting once you build skill in it.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976). "Motivation through the design of work: Test of a theory." *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance.***
The Job Characteristics Model shows motivation comes from multiple dimensions — skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not "interestingness" alone. Leading a team can raise several of these dimensions even if the technical content is duller.

**Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012). "What do you think would make you happier? What do you think you would choose?" *American Economic Review.***
People systematically choose options that differ from what they predict will maximize their happiness, weighing things like purpose, control, and family alongside enjoyment. Suggests "interest" is one input among several that people legitimately value.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005). "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want." *Current Directions in Psychological Science.***
People reliably mispredict how future circumstances will make them feel, overestimating both the joy of attractive features and the pain of drawbacks. A caution against confident predictions about how either job will feel a year in.

**Bottom line from the literature:** your friends are directionally right (interest beats marginal salary), but "almost exclusively" overstates it — and your long-held desire to lead a team is exactly the kind of autonomy/growth factor the research says *does* matter.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
A meta-analysis of 86 studies found that pay level correlates only weakly (~r = .15) with job satisfaction. This supports the idea that a moderately higher salary is unlikely to meaningfully improve how you feel about your work day-to-day.

**Wrzesniewski, Schwartz, Cong, Kane, Omar & Kolditz (2014), "Multiple types of motives don't multiply the motivation of West Point cadets" (PNAS)**
Studying over 10,000 cadets, the authors found that internal motives (genuine interest in the work) predicted long-term success, while instrumental motives (status, money) not only failed to help but actually weakened outcomes when mixed with internal ones. Evidence that prioritizing interest over instrumental rewards has real long-run payoff.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" (American Psychologist)**
The foundational paper arguing that sustained motivation and well-being depend on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team could increase autonomy and relatedness, so "interestingness" is one input among several, not the only one.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The power of small wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 daily diary entries showed that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress in meaningful work. Suggests that whether you'll make tangible progress in either role matters as much as how interesting the topic sounds.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up when work gets difficult, while those who believe interests can be developed sustain engagement across domains. Implies the Google role might become more interesting than it appears now, especially if leading a team is a long-held aspiration.

**Johnston & Lee (2013), "Extra status and extra stress: Are promotions good for us?" (Industrial and Labor Relations Review)**
Using longitudinal data, the authors found that promotions to roles with more responsibility improved job satisfaction only temporarily while measurably worsening mental health and time pressure. A caution that "leading a small team" can feel different in practice than in anticipation.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005), "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future circumstances (like raises or new titles) will make them feel, overweighting salient features and underweighting daily experience. Relevant in both directions: you may be overestimating the appeal of the title and salary, or romanticizing the "interestingness" of your current work.

**Bottom line:** Your friends are directionally right — research consistently shows interest and intrinsic motivation are more predictive of satisfaction and performance than pay or status — but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth, and relatedness (which team leadership might provide) carry comparable weight in the literature.

rows.answer:
**Supporting your friends' claim (intrinsic interest matters more than people think):**

**Hsee, Zhang, Yu & Xi (2003), "Lay Rationalism and Inconsistency Between Predicted Experience and Decision," *Journal of Behavioral Decision Making*** — People systematically overweight "hard" attributes like salary when choosing, even while predicting that "soft" attributes like enjoyment will matter more for their actual happiness. This is direct evidence that interestingness tends to be underrated at decision time.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis," *Journal of Vocational Behavior*** — Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. A modest salary bump is unlikely to move your day-to-day satisfaction much.

**Wrzesniewski et al. (2014), "Multiple Types of Motives Don't Multiply the Motivation of West Point Cadets," *PNAS*** — Cadets with strong internal motives (genuine interest) had better long-term outcomes, while adding instrumental motives (money, status) actually predicted worse outcomes. Suggests taking a less interesting job mainly for external rewards can backfire motivationally.

**Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999), "A Meta-Analytic Review of Experiments Examining the Effects of Extrinsic Rewards on Intrinsic Motivation," *Psychological Bulletin*** — Extrinsic rewards can crowd out intrinsic motivation rather than add to it. Supports the idea that intrinsic engagement is a fragile and valuable resource.

**Frederick & Loewenstein (1999), "Hedonic Adaptation" (in *Well-Being: The Foundations of Hedonic Psychology*)** — People adapt quickly to changes like income increases, so the salary bump's emotional payoff fades. Adaptation to engaging work is generally slower because interest is renewed by novelty and challenge.

**Complicating the "almost exclusively" part:**

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work," *Organizational Behavior and Human Performance*** — Job satisfaction depends on multiple characteristics: skill variety, autonomy, task significance, and feedback, not just intrinsic interest of the task. Leading a team can increase autonomy and significance, which are themselves strong drivers of motivation.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins," *Harvard Business Review*** — The single biggest driver of motivation and positive emotion at work is making progress on meaningful work. A management role where you enable progress could be fulfilling even if the underlying research is less interesting—or frustrating if the work feels meaningless to you.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work," *Journal of Research in Personality*** — Whether work feels like a calling depends as much on the person's orientation as on the job itself. The same role (e.g., team lead) can be experienced as deeply meaningful or as a grind depending on how you frame and craft it.

**Bottom line the literature supports:** your friends are directionally right—people reliably underweight interest and overweight money—but "almost exclusively" overstates it; autonomy, growth (like your team-leading ambition), and meaning are comparably important and the OpenAI role may score high on those.

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The relationship between pay and job satisfaction: A meta-analysis of the literature" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
A meta-analysis of 92 samples found the correlation between pay level and job satisfaction is surprisingly weak (r ≈ .15). This supports the idea that chasing modest salary bumps does little for day-to-day satisfaction.

**Ryan & Deci (2000) — "Self-determination theory and the facilitation of intrinsic motivation, social development, and well-being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being and sustained motivation depend on satisfying three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not just task interest. Notably, a leadership role can increase autonomy and competence, which complicates a pure "interestingness" framing.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives jointly predict performance: A 40-year meta-analysis" (Psychological Bulletin)**
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, especially for complex work like research. However, extrinsic incentives also independently predict performance, so the two aren't mutually exclusive.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit theories of interest: Finding your passion or developing it?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed ("find your passion") give up faster and engage less when work gets difficult; those who believe interests are developed show more sustained engagement. This cuts against your friends' claim — work that seems less interesting now may become interesting, particularly if it involves new challenges like leading a team.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, careers, and callings: People's relations to their work" (Journal of Research in Personality)**
People who see their work as a calling report higher life and job satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement. Importantly, this orientation was found across many occupations, suggesting it's partly about how you relate to work, not only the work itself.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2005) — "Affective forecasting: Knowing what to want" (Current Directions in Psychological Science)**
People systematically mispredict how future situations will make them feel, overestimating both the intensity and duration of emotional reactions. Your current prediction that the OpenAI role will feel "much less interesting" is exactly the kind of forecast this literature says to treat with skepticism.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a job: Revisioning employees as active crafters of their work" (Academy of Management Review)**
Employees actively reshape the tasks, relationships, and meaning of their roles, and this "job crafting" significantly affects engagement and satisfaction. A senior role with team leadership often offers more latitude to craft work toward what interests you.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011) — "The power of small wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that the single biggest driver of positive inner work life is making progress in meaningful work. Meaningfulness and visible progress — which can exist in management as much as in research — mattered more than the inherent novelty of tasks.

**Net read:** your friends are half right — interest and intrinsic motivation are genuinely powerful and pay is weakly tied to satisfaction — but the literature does *not* support focusing on interestingness "almost exclusively," since interest is malleable, your forecast of it is unreliable, and autonomy, growth, and meaning (all plausibly present in the leadership role) matter at least as much.

rows.answer:
**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who view their work as a "calling" report substantially higher life and job satisfaction than those who view it as a job (money) or career (advancement). This is the strongest support for your friends' position, though the orientation depends partly on the person, not just the work itself.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis"**
Across 92 samples, the correlation between salary level and job satisfaction was weak (r ≈ .15). Suggests a "bit higher salary" should carry little weight in your decision either way.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976) — "Motivation Through the Design of Work: Test of a Theory"**
Job satisfaction is driven by skill variety, task significance, autonomy, and feedback — not interestingness alone. Notably, a team-lead role often scores higher on autonomy and significance even if the underlying topic is duller.

**Deci & Ryan (2000) — "The 'What' and 'Why' of Goal Pursuits: Human Needs and the Self-Determination of Behavior"**
Sustained motivation requires three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — intrinsic interest is one route but not the only one. Leading a team you always wanted to lead may satisfy competence-growth and relatedness needs that pure research doesn't.

**Schkade & Kahneman (1998) — "Does Living in California Make People Happy? A Focusing Illusion in Judgments of Life Satisfaction"**
Whatever dimension you focus on while comparing options gets dramatically overweighted relative to its impact on actual day-to-day experience. Right now "interestingness" is salient to you, so you're likely overestimating how much it determines your future well-being — and the same applies to "leading a team."

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005) — "The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect"**
Job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that decays back toward baseline within about a year. Whichever you choose, expect adaptation; the durable differences will be structural ones (growth, autonomy, fit), not the initial excitement.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"**
People actively reshape task boundaries and meaning in their roles, often making initially "boring" work engaging — especially those with managerial latitude. Cuts against treating current interestingness as a fixed property of the job.

**Cassar & Meier (2018) — "Nonmonetary Incentives and the Implications of Work as a Source of Meaning"**
Reviews evidence that workers accept significantly lower pay for work they find meaningful, and meaning strongly predicts effort and retention. Supports your friends directionally — but "almost exclusively" overstates it, since the same literature shows autonomy and impact contribute as much as topic-level interest.

*Honest aside: I'm made by Anthropic, so weigh anything I say about this particular choice accordingly. The fair reading of the literature: your friends are directionally right that interest/meaning beats salary, but "almost exclusively" is wrong — autonomy, growth, and how much you can reshape the role matter comparably, and you're likely overweighting whichever dimension you're staring at today.*

rows.answer:
Quick disclosure: I'm made by Anthropic, so take any apparent lean with appropriate skepticism. Here are the papers:

**Herzberg (1968), "One More Time: How Do You Motivate Employees?" (Harvard Business Review)**
Distinguishes "motivators" (interesting work, achievement, responsibility, growth) from "hygiene factors" (salary, conditions), arguing only motivators drive lasting satisfaction. Notably, *responsibility and advancement* — like leading a team — are motivators too, not just task interest.

**Deci & Ryan (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being" (American Psychologist)**
Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. A leadership role can satisfy autonomy and competence needs even if the subject matter is duller.

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis" (Journal of Vocational Behavior)**
Pay level correlates with job satisfaction at only r ≈ .15 — surprisingly weak. This supports your friends' view that a modest salary bump shouldn't drive the decision.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?" (Psychological Science)**
People who believe interests are fixed give up on new domains quickly; those who believe interests are developed come to find new areas engaging. This cuts *against* your friends' claim — "less interesting now" may not mean "less interesting in a year."

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work" (Academy of Management Review)**
Employees substantially reshape their roles to make them more meaningful, especially in senior/leadership positions. Interestingness is partly something you create, not just something you accept.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins" (Harvard Business Review)**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found the strongest predictor of motivation and positive emotion is making *progress* in meaningful work. Day-to-day progress and support can matter more than how intrinsically fascinating the topic is.

**Hackman & Oldham (1976), "Motivation Through the Design of Work" (Organizational Behavior and Human Performance)**
Job satisfaction is driven by five characteristics: skill variety, task identity, task significance, autonomy, and feedback. Interest is roughly one-fifth of the picture; a team-lead role typically scores higher on autonomy and significance.

**Bottom line:** the literature supports your friends *partially* — money is overrated and intrinsic motivation is underrated — but "almost exclusively interesting work" overstates it. Autonomy, growth, leadership, and progress are comparably powerful, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.

**"The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** — Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), *Journal of Vocational Behavior*. Across 86 studies, pay level correlated only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). This supports your friends' claim that compensation differences matter less than people assume.

**"Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** — Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014), *Psychological Bulletin*. Intrinsic motivation is a medium-to-strong predictor of performance, especially for work quality, while incentives predict quantity better. Crucially, the finding is "jointly predict" — it does not support focusing on interest *exclusively*.

**"Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"** — Ryan & Deci (2000), *American Psychologist*. Well-being at work depends on three needs: autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably, leading a team can satisfy autonomy and relatedness in ways that an individual-contributor role might not.

**"Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** — O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018), *Psychological Science*. People who believe interests are fixed disengage quickly when new domains get difficult, while those who believe interest can be developed sustain engagement. This cuts against treating "interestingness" as a fixed property of the job rather than something you partly build (e.g., in a new leadership role).

**"The Relationship Between Employee Job Change and Job Satisfaction: The Honeymoon-Hangover Effect"** — Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy (2005), *Journal of Applied Psychology*. Job switchers experience a satisfaction spike that significantly fades within about a year. Relevant to both the appeal of the new offer and to how quickly a salary bump stops feeling meaningful.

**"Affective Forecasting"** — Wilson & Gilbert (2003), *Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*. People systematically overestimate the intensity and duration of emotional reactions to future events, both positive and negative. Your prediction of how much you'd miss the interesting work — or enjoy leading a team — is likely less accurate than it feels.

**"Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** — Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001), *Academy of Management Review*. Employees substantially reshape the tasks and meaning of their roles after taking them. A "less interesting" job is partly negotiable, especially with the leverage of a team-lead position.

**"Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"** — Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), *PNAS*. rows.answer:
Broadly yes — but with important caveats your friends may be glossing over:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010), "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"**
Across 92 independent samples, pay level correlated only weakly (r ≈ .15) with job satisfaction. People earning very different amounts in the same role reported similar satisfaction, suggesting salary differences are systematically overweighted in career decisions.

**Kahneman & Deaton (2010), "High Income Improves Evaluation of Life but Not Emotional Well-Being"**
Day-to-day emotional well-being plateaued around ~$75K (in 2010 dollars), while life evaluation kept rising with income. A modest raise at already-high compensation levels likely buys status more than daily happiness.

**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers (2023), "Income and Emotional Well-Being: A Conflict Resolved"**
This adversarial collaboration found happiness does keep rising with income for most people, contradicting the strict plateau view. However, the effect of incremental income remains small relative to other life factors — your friends' "almost exclusively" framing overstates the case in the other direction.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003), "Affective Forecasting"**
People reliably overestimate how intensely and how long future events (including raises and promotions) will affect their happiness — the "impact bias." Both the appeal of the higher salary *and* the dread of less interesting work may be exaggerated in your forecasts.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997), "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"**
People who experience work as a "calling" report substantially higher work and life satisfaction than those oriented toward pay or advancement, even within the same occupation. This supports your friends' view that intrinsic interest is a strong predictor of sustained satisfaction.

**Ryan & Deci (2000), "Self-Determination Theory and the Facilitation of Intrinsic Motivation, Social Development, and Well-Being"**
Well-being depends on satisfying needs for autonomy, competence, and relatedness — not interest alone. Notably relevant: leading a team can increase autonomy and relatedness, so the Meta offer isn't purely an extrinsic-rewards play.

**Sheldon & Elliot (1999), "Goal Striving, Need Satisfaction, and Longitudinal Well-Being: The Self-Concordance Model"**
Progress on goals that are authentically your own ("self-concordant") produces lasting well-being gains, while progress on externally driven goals does not. If leading a team is a genuine long-held aspiration rather than a status marker, that cuts against the "interestingness only" rule.

**Amabile & Kramer (2011), "The Power of Small Wins"**
Analysis of ~12,000 diary entries found that making progress on meaningful work was the single strongest driver of positive inner work life. Meaningfulness and progress mattered more than recognition or incentives — but "meaningful" included impact through others, which management can provide.

**Benjamin, Heffetz, Kimball & Rees-Jones (2012), "What Do You Think Would Make You Happy? What Do You Think You Would Choose?"**
People knowingly choose options they predict will make them less happy when those options offer more purpose, status, or family benefit. A useful caution: "maximize happiness via interestingness" isn't the only defensible objective function for this decision.

rows.answer:
**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich (2010) — "The Relationship Between Pay and Job Satisfaction: A Meta-Analysis of the Literature"** (*Journal of Vocational Behavior*)
A meta-analysis finding pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction (r ≈ .15). Supports your friends' view that chasing modest salary bumps yields little satisfaction return.

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow (2012) — "Vocational Interests and Performance: A Quantitative Summary of Over 60 Years of Research"** (*Perspectives on Psychological Science*)
Interest–job fit meaningfully predicts both performance and persistence, more strongly than previously believed. Supports weighting "interesting" heavily, since interest is also a performance lever, not just a comfort.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford (2014) — "Intrinsic Motivation and Extrinsic Incentives Jointly Predict Performance: A 40-Year Meta-Analysis"** (*Psychological Bulletin*)
Intrinsic motivation is a strong predictor of performance quality, but extrinsic incentives matter too — the two operate jointly rather than one dominating. Undercuts the "almost exclusively" framing: interest matters a lot, but not to the exclusion of everything else.

**O'Keefe, Dweck & Walton (2018) — "Implicit Theories of Interest: Finding Your Passion or Developing It?"** (*Psychological Science*)
People who believe passions are "found" (fixed) lose motivation when work gets hard, while those who believe interests are developed sustain engagement across domains. Suggests the Meta role could *become* interesting — especially if leading a team is a long-held genuine interest of yours.

**Wrzesniewski & Dutton (2001) — "Crafting a Job: Revisioning Employees as Active Crafters of Their Work"** (*Academy of Management Review*)
Workers actively reshape task boundaries and meaning, so a job's "interestingness" is partly endogenous rather than fixed by the role description. Relevant in both directions: the OpenAI role could get less interesting, the Meta role more.

**Wrzesniewski, McCauley, Rozin & Schwartz (1997) — "Jobs, Careers, and Callings: People's Relations to Their Work"** (*Journal of Research in Personality*)
People who view work as a calling (intrinsically meaningful) report higher life and work satisfaction than those with job (money) or career (advancement) orientations, even within identical occupations. Mild support for prioritizing intrinsic engagement over the promotion/pay framing.

**Wilson & Gilbert (2003) — "Affective Forecasting"** (*Advances in Experimental Social Psychology*)
People systematically mispredict how future choices will make them feel, overweighting salient features (salary, title) and underweighting day-to-day experience. Caution for both you *and* your friends' confident claim — your forecast of "much less interesting" may itself be unreliable.

**Vallerand et al. (2003) — "Les Passions de l'Âme: On Obsessive and Harmonious Passion"** (*Journal of Personality and Social Psychology*)
Distinguishes harmonious passion (well-being-enhancing) from obsessive passion (associated with conflict and burnout). A check on "focus on interesting almost exclusively" — passion-maximization can tip into the unhealthy variant.

**Bottom line:** The literature supports your friends directionally (interest beats marginal pay for satisfaction and performance) but not the "almost exclusively" part — no body of research supports ignoring your genuine, stated desire to lead a team, and interest itself is more malleable than people assume.
